perturbation.original_text: Unless the parties agree otherwise, this obligation of confidentiality will expire two (2) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless the parties agree otherwise, this obligation of confidentiality will expire one (1) year from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with laws designed to safeguard proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides legal recourse for misappropriation of trade secrets. While CUTSA doesn't specify a minimum confidentiality period, a one-year period might be deemed unreasonably short to protect certain trade secrets, making it difficult to prove reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially harming a trade secret claim. This is particularly true for industries with long product lifecycles. Modifying the confidentiality period could affect a party's ability to seek legal remedies under CUTSA if trade secrets are misappropriated.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from two years to one year. The Law Explanation states this may be 'unreasonably short to protect certain trade secrets, making it difficult to prove reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy'. The Perturbation Explanation indicates a conflict 'with laws designed to safeguard proprietary information'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites 'California Code, CIV 3426' as the 'Uniform Trade Secrets Act', indicating the state's recognition of trade secret protection. While the law does not specify a minimum confidentiality period, shortening it could undermine efforts to safeguard proprietary information, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4 Notices Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent by a) registered mail; b) tracked overnight carrier or c) email transmission where the recipient specifically replies to acknowledge receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4 Notices Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent only by standard mail.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice delivery to standard mail conflicts with common legal requirements for timely and verifiable notice in contractual matters.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 outlines methods for service by mail and extends response times based on location of service. By restricting notice to standard mail only, the modified clause fails to account for the additional time allowed for responses when service is by mail, potentially violating due process and fair notice principles if critical deadlines are triggered without adequate time for response, especially when out-of-state parties are involved.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of Californi
perturbation.justification: The modified text restricts notice delivery to standard mail, conflicting with California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013. CCP 1013(a) specifies that service by mail extends response times depending on the location of service. By limiting notice to standard mail, the contract fails to account for these extended response times, potentially violating due process as parties may not have adequate time to respond to notices, thus the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.5 Governing Law This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its choice of laws rules.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.5 Governing Law This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its choice of laws rules, regardless of the parties' locations or activities.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying Delaware law while the company is in California could conflict with California's interest in protecting its businesses and may not be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.10 grants California courts jurisdiction over individuals or entities to the extent permitted by the U.S. Constitution. Selecting Delaware law despite the agreement being executed and performed primarily in California, and the parties' presence there, could be challenged if it attempts to circumvent California's laws and policies related to business conduct within the state. California courts may refuse to enforce the Delaware choice-of-law provision if it violates a strong California public policy. This alteration could lead to jurisdictional disputes and uncertainty regarding the agreement's enforceability in California.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that selecting Delaware law, despite the agreement being executed and performed primarily in California, could be challenged if it attempts to circumvent California's laws and policies. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CCP 410.10, states that a California court may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. Choosing Delaware law could be seen as an attempt to avoid California jurisdiction, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.9 Termination Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time upon written notice to the other party. Promptly after such termination, all obligations of Participant and Khronos under this Agreement will terminate, and both parties will return or destroy all materials provided by the other party pursuant to th is Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the rights and obligations set forth in Sections 4 (Confidential Information), 5.7 (No Warranty) and 5.8 (Limitation of Liability) will survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.9 Termination Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time with no prior notice to the other party. Upon such termination, all obligations of Participant and Khronos under this Agreement will terminate immediately, including the return or destruction of materials.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the notice requirement for termination and eliminating the survival clause for confidentiality could violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1203 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Eliminating the notice requirement for termination and the survival of confidentiality obligations could be interpreted as a breach of this implied duty, especially if one party relies on the continued confidentiality or the reasonable expectation of continued performance. A court could find that the sudden termination without notice and the immediate extinguishment of all obligations, including confidentiality, violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, rendering the termination clause unenforceable or subject to damages.

General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1203. (a) Whether a transaction in the form of a lease creates a lease or security interest is determined by the facts of each case. (b) A transaction in the form of a lease creates a security interest if the consideration that the lessee is to pay the lessor for the right to possession and use of the goods is an obligation for the term of the lease and is not subject to termination by the lessee, and: (1) the original term of the lease is equal to or greater than the remaining economic life of the goods; (2) the lessee is bound to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods or is bound to become the owner of the goods; (3) the lessee has an option to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement; or (4) the lessee has an option to become the owner of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement of prior notice for termination and eliminates the survival clause for confidentiality. The Law Explanation suggests this could breach the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under California Commercial Code § 1203. The scraped snippet contains California Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 1, CHAPTER 2, and references 1203, further supporting the relevance of this law. The removal of the notice and confidentiality obligations directly contradicts the implied duty, making the contract potentially unenforceable or subject to damages.
